title: VN — Value Network: Finfish Farming and Fish Hatcheries (NAICS 112511)

sections.title: Header
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|

| **CFJ** | Cultivate finfish from egg or fingerling to market-size harvest in a controlled aquatic environment |
| **Output Types** | Coldwater RAS (salmon, trout, char); Warmwater RAS (tilapia, barramundi, perch, sturgeon); Hatchery / Fingerling; Flow-Through / Raceway; Pond / Earthen |

| **Market Size** | ~$5.2B globally (integrated RAS, 2024); projected $13B by 2034 at 9.6% CAGR; ~$472M filtration equipment [SRC: web_search] |

sections.title: L7 — Ecosystem
sections.content:
### L7: Finfish Aquaculture Ecosystem

The complete ecosystem of interdependent fish farmers, hatcheries, equipment OEMs, feed manufacturers, genetics/broodstock suppliers, veterinary services, processing plants, distributors, and regulators (USDA APHIS, FDA, state aquaculture agencies, EU EFSA) coordinated around the land-based and nearshore cultivation of finfish. NAICS 112511 covers ~3,200 U.S. establishments. Global aquaculture overtook wild-catch in 2022 and now produces >50% of consumed fish. The integrated RAS equipment market reached $5.2B in 2024 with a 9.6% CAGR driven by land-based salmon projects, sustainability mandates, and biosecurity concerns. [SRC: web_search, FAO, USDA Census]

---

sections.title: L6 — System-of-Systems Decomposition
sections.content:
Nine L6 core process steps follow the physical transformation sequence from water intake to harvested fish. Four L6 horizontal steps run in parallel across all core steps.

| L6 ID | Name | Type | Job Family |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L6.1** | Water Intake & Conditioning | Core | Source, condition, and deliver water at required quality for rearing |
| **L6.2** | Mechanical Filtration | Core | Remove particulate solids (feces, uneaten feed) from rearing water |
| **L6.3** | Biological Filtration | Core | Convert toxic ammonia and nitrite to less-toxic nitrate via nitrifying bacteria |
| **L6.4** | Gas Management | Core | Strip CO2, inject dissolved oxygen to maintain respirable water |
| **L6.5** | Disinfection | Core | Kill pathogens (bacteria, viruses, parasites) in recirculating water |
| **L6.6** | Temperature Control | Core | Maintain species-specific water temperature for optimal growth |
| **L6.7** | Fish Rearing | Core | House fish from fingerling to harvest weight in controlled tanks |
| **L6.8** | Feed Delivery | Core | Distribute formulated feed to fish at optimized rates and schedules |
| **L6.9** | Harvest & Grading | Core | Remove market-size fish from rearing units, sort by size and quality |
| **L6.10** | Water Quality Monitoring & Control (H) | Horizontal | Continuously sense DO, pH, ammonia, temperature, flow across all steps. **MARQUARDT PRIMARY POSITION.** |
| **L6.11** | Biosecurity & Fish Health (H) | Horizontal | Quarantine, vaccinate, and monitor disease across all rearing units |
| **L6.12** | Waste & Effluent Management (H) | Horizontal | Treat and discharge or reuse waste streams from filtration and harvest |
| **L6.13** | Facility Utilities & Power (H) | Horizontal | Provide electrical power, emergency backup, compressed air, and building services |

---

sections.title: L6.1 — Water Intake & Conditioning
sections.content:
First step in the chain. Water quality at intake determines chemical and biological load on all downstream treatment stages. Municipal, well, or surface water each require different conditioning.

### L5 Systems under L6.1

#### L5.1.1: Source Water Pump Station

Lifts and delivers raw water from well, municipal supply, or surface source to the conditioning stage.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.1.1: Intake Pump | Centrifugal or submersible pump; 10–200 m³/h capacity depending on farm scale |
| L4.1.1.2: Intake Screen | Coarse bar screen or wedge-wire screen preventing debris entry |
| L4.1.1.3: Variable Speed Drive | VFD on pump motor for flow regulation and energy savings |

#### L5.1.2: Pre-Filtration

Removes suspended sediment and large particulates before water enters the rearing loop.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.2.1: Sand Filter | Pressurized rapid sand filter; 50–100 μm retention |
| L4.1.2.2: Sediment Trap | Gravity settling basin for heavy particulates |
| L4.1.2.3: Backwash Valve Assembly | Automated multiport valve for periodic filter backwash |

#### L5.1.3: Water Chemistry Adjustment

Conditions intake water to species-specific requirements for pH, alkalinity, and hardness.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.3.1: pH Dosing System | Metering pump + CO2 injection or acid/base dosing |
| L4.1.3.2: Alkalinity Buffer Dosing | Sodium bicarbonate or calcium carbonate dosing for nitrification support |
| L4.1.3.3: Dechlorination Unit | Activated carbon filter or sodium thiosulfate injection for municipal water |

---

sections.title: L6.2 — Mechanical Filtration
sections.content:
Primary solids removal. Must capture >95% of suspended solids to protect biofilter media from clogging. Drum filters dominate RAS installations (~70% market share). [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.2

#### L5.2.1: Drum Filter

Dominant mechanical filtration technology in commercial RAS. Rotating drum screen with pressurized spray backwash. Screen mesh 40–100 μm. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.1.1: Rotating Drum Screen | Stainless steel or polyester mesh drum; 40–100 μm aperture |
| L4.2.1.2: Spray Nozzle Assembly | Pressurized spray bar cleaning screen during rotation |
| L4.2.1.3: Drive Motor & Gearbox | Low-speed gear motor rotating drum; 0.5–2 RPM |

#### L5.2.2: Radial Flow Settler

Gravity-based particle separation for heavier settleable solids. Often used as primary removal ahead of drum filter.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.2.1: Settler Cone/Vessel | Conical-bottom tank; 60° cone angle for gravity settling |
| L4.2.2.2: Sludge Drain Valve | Automated knife-gate or pinch valve for periodic sludge discharge |
| L4.2.2.3: Baffle Assembly | Internal baffles directing flow for laminar settling conditions |

#### L5.2.3: Protein Skimmer / Foam Fractionator

Removes dissolved organic compounds and fine suspended particles via foam flotation. Critical for marine and high-density freshwater systems.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.3.1: Venturi Injector / Air Pump | Creates fine bubble column for foam formation |
| L4.2.3.2: Contact Chamber | Tall reaction column where bubbles aggregate organics into foam |
| L4.2.3.3: Collection Cup | Overflow cup capturing concentrated foam/waste for disposal |

---

sections.title: L6.3 — Biological Filtration
sections.content:
The defining step of RAS — without bioconversion, ammonia from fish metabolism reaches lethal levels within hours. Autotrophic nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas, Nitrospira) colonize high-surface-area media. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.3

#### L5.3.1: Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR)

Dominant biofilter type in modern RAS. Neutrally buoyant plastic media (Kaldnes K1, BioElements) suspended by aeration. Specific surface area 500–800 m²/m³. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.1.1: Reactor Vessel | HDPE or concrete tank sized for hydraulic retention time |
| L4.3.1.2: Bio-Media | Kaldnes K1/K3, BioElements, or equivalent plastic carrier media |
| L4.3.1.3: Aeration Grid | Coarse-bubble diffuser grid keeping media in suspension |
| L4.3.1.4: Retention Screen | Wedge-wire or slotted screen preventing media loss to downstream |

#### L5.3.2: Trickling Tower / Biotower

Passive-aeration biofilter. Water trickles over structured media while air flows counter-current. Also serves as CO2 stripper. Common in smaller RAS. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.2.1: Distribution Plate | Rotating or static spray plate distributing water over media surface |
| L4.3.2.2: Structured Media Pack | Polypropylene or PVC cross-flow media; 150–250 m²/m³ surface area |
| L4.3.2.3: Sump Collection Basin | Basin below tower collecting treated water for return to system |

#### L5.3.3: Fluidized Sand Biofilter

High-efficiency biofilter using fine sand (0.2–0.5 mm) as media. Superior nitrification rate per volume but higher energy cost.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.3.1: Filter Column | Vertical cylindrical vessel; upflow design |
| L4.3.3.2: Sand Media | Fine silica sand providing ~5,000 m²/m³ specific surface area |
| L4.3.3.3: Expansion / Recirculation Pump | Pump maintaining sand bed in fluidized state at 20–50% expansion |

---

sections.title: L6.4 — Gas Management
sections.content:
Dissolved oxygen (DO) must stay above 6 mg/L (coldwater) or 4 mg/L (warmwater). CO2 must stay below 15–20 mg/L. In high-density RAS, pure oxygen supplementation is standard; ambient aeration alone is insufficient. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.4

#### L5.4.1: Oxygen Supply

Pure oxygen delivery to the facility. On-site generation (PSA/VPSA) for large farms; liquid oxygen (LOX) for smaller or backup.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.1.1: PSA / VPSA Oxygen Generator | Pressure or vacuum swing adsorption producing 90–95% O2 from ambient air |
| L4.4.1.2: LOX Storage Tank | Cryogenic liquid oxygen tank with vaporizer; backup or primary for small sites |
| L4.4.1.3: Pressure Regulator & Distribution Manifold | Reduces O2 pressure and distributes to injection points per tank zone |

#### L5.4.2: Oxygen Injection

Dissolves pure oxygen into rearing water at high transfer efficiency.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.2.1: Low-Head Oxygenator (LHO) | Gravity-driven packed column; >90% O2 transfer efficiency; dominant in RAS [SRC: web_search] |
| L4.4.2.2: Oxygen Cone / Speece Cone | Pressurized downflow contactor; high transfer in small footprint |
| L4.4.2.3: Diffuser Stone / Fine-Bubble Diffuser | Ceramic or membrane diffuser for tank-level supplemental aeration |

#### L5.4.3: CO2 Stripper / Degasser

Removes dissolved carbon dioxide from recirculating water. Often combined with trickling tower biofilter.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.3.1: Packed Column | Counter-current air/water contactor; cascade or random packing |
| L4.4.3.2: Forced-Draft Blower | Fan driving ambient air upward through the column |
| L4.4.3.3: Sump Pump | Returns degassed water from column sump to main loop |

---

sections.title: L6.5 — Disinfection
sections.content:
Pathogen control in the recirculating loop. UV-C is dominant due to chemical-free operation and no residual effects on biofilter bacteria. Ozone used in high-biosecurity operations (hatcheries, smolt units). [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.5

#### L5.5.1: UV Sterilizer

UV-C irradiation destroying bacteria, viruses, and parasites. Sized by UV dose (40–100 mJ/cm² depending on target pathogen). Standard in nearly all RAS.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.1.1: UV-C Lamp Array | Low-pressure or medium-pressure mercury lamps; 254 nm wavelength |
| L4.5.1.2: Quartz Sleeve | Transparent protective tube around each lamp; periodic cleaning required |
| L4.5.1.3: Ballast / Driver | Electronic ballast controlling lamp power; UV intensity sensor feedback |

#### L5.5.2: Ozone Treatment

Ozone (O3) injection for advanced disinfection and micro-flocculation. Requires careful dosing — excess ozone is toxic to fish. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.2.1: Ozone Generator | Corona discharge or UV-generated ozone; 10–500 g/h capacity |
| L4.5.2.2: Contact Chamber | Pressurized or atmospheric ozone/water contactor with retention time |
| L4.5.2.3: ORP Sensor | Oxidation-reduction potential sensor controlling ozone dose to safe levels |

---

sections.title: L6.6 — Temperature Control
sections.content:
Species-specific temperature windows determine growth rate, feed conversion, and disease susceptibility. Coldwater salmonids: 10–16 °C. Warmwater tilapia: 26–30 °C. Temperature stability ±1 °C is critical for spawning and juvenile stages. **Marquardt secondary position — flow metering on chiller/heater water loops.** [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.6

#### L5.6.1: Heat Exchanger

Transfers thermal energy between rearing water and the heating/cooling source without mixing fluids. Plate or shell-and-tube designs.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.1.1: Plate Heat Exchanger | Titanium or 316L stainless plates; high efficiency; compact footprint |
| L4.6.1.2: Gasket Set | EPDM or Viton gaskets preventing cross-contamination |
| L4.6.1.3: Mounting Frame | Bolted frame with tightening torque specification |

#### L5.6.2: Chiller / Heat Pump

Active cooling for coldwater species or tropical facility climate control. Scroll or screw compressor with R-410A or R-134a refrigerant.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.2.1: Compressor | Scroll or screw compressor; 20–500 kW cooling capacity |
| L4.6.2.2: Condenser | Air-cooled or water-cooled condenser rejecting waste heat |
| L4.6.2.3: Expansion Valve | Thermostatic or electronic expansion valve controlling superheat |

#### L5.6.3: Heating System

Heats water for warmwater species or maintains hatchery spawning temperature. Boiler, electric heater, or heat pump in reverse mode.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.3.1: Boiler / Electric Heater | Gas-fired boiler or immersion heater; 10–300 kW |
| L4.6.3.2: Circulation Pump | Hot water loop pump delivering heated water to heat exchanger |
| L4.6.3.3: Mixing Valve | Three-way proportional valve blending heated and return water to setpoint |

---

sections.title: L6.7 — Fish Rearing
sections.content:
The core transformation step — where fish grow from fingerling to market weight. Tank hydrodynamics, water exchange rate, and stocking density determine growth efficiency and fish welfare. RAS tanks are typically circular for self-cleaning flow patterns. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.7

#### L5.7.1: Grow-Out Tank

Primary rearing vessel for market-size production. Circular tanks (6–15 m diameter) in RAS; rectangular raceways in flow-through systems. Stocking density 40–100 kg/m³ for RAS salmonids.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.1.1: Tank Vessel | Fiberglass, HDPE, or concrete circular tank; 50–500 m³ volume |
| L4.7.1.2: Center Drain Assembly | Dual-drain with bottom drain (solids) and side drain (clean water) |
| L4.7.1.3: Standpipe / Water Level Control | Adjustable standpipe or overflow weir setting tank water level |

#### L5.7.2: Fry / Fingerling Tank

Smaller nursery tanks for juvenile stages (1–50 g). Require gentler water currents and finer mesh screens to prevent escape.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.2.1: Nursery Tank | Small circular or rectangular tank; 1–10 m³; smooth interior |
| L4.7.2.2: Fine-Mesh Screen | 0.5–2 mm mesh preventing fry escape at outlet |
| L4.7.2.3: Gentle Aeration System | Low-velocity air diffuser or surface agitator for DO without fry stress |

#### L5.7.3: Tank Hydrodynamics

Systems creating self-cleaning water flow patterns that sweep solids to center drain while distributing oxygen uniformly.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.3.1: Inlet Spray Bar | Tangential inlet creating rotational flow for self-cleaning |
| L4.7.3.2: Current Generator | Supplemental pump or propeller for large tanks requiring additional circulation |
| L4.7.3.3: Dead-Zone Prevention Baffle | Internal baffles eliminating stagnant zones where solids accumulate |

---

sections.title: L6.8 — Feed Delivery
sections.content:
Feed is the largest operating cost in aquaculture (40–60% of total cost). Feed conversion ratio (FCR) optimization directly determines profitability. Automated feeding with waste-feed detection reduces overfeeding and nutrient loading on biofilters. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.8

#### L5.8.1: Automatic Feeder

Delivers pelletized feed to tanks on programmable schedules. Pneumatic, belt, or screw feeder types.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.1.1: Feed Hopper | Stainless or PE hopper; 50–500 kg capacity per tank |
| L4.8.1.2: Dosing Mechanism | Screw auger, vibrating tray, or pneumatic blower for precise portion control |
| L4.8.1.3: Timer / Controller | PLC or standalone timer controlling feed frequency and portion size |

#### L5.8.2: Feed Storage & Handling

Bulk storage and distribution of formulated feed from delivery to feeder hopper.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.2.1: Feed Silo | Outdoor bulk silo; 5–50 t capacity; vermin-proof |
| L4.8.2.2: Pneumatic Conveyor | Air-driven transport from silo to individual feeder hoppers |
| L4.8.2.3: Moisture & Temperature Monitor | Sensors preventing feed spoilage from moisture absorption |

#### L5.8.3: Feed Monitoring

Detects uneaten feed and optimizes feeding regime for feed conversion efficiency.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.3.1: Waste Feed Detector | Infrared or acoustic sensor at tank drain detecting uneaten pellets |
| L4.8.3.2: Camera Vision System | Underwater or above-water camera with AI-driven appetite assessment |
| L4.8.3.3: FCR Logger | Software module tracking feed input vs biomass gain per tank |

---

sections.title: L6.9 — Harvest & Grading
sections.content:
Final step in the rearing cycle. Fish must be graded by size, humanely slaughtered, and transferred to processing or live transport. Animal welfare regulations increasingly mandate stunning before slaughter. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.9

#### L5.9.1: Grading Machine

Sorts fish by size for uniform batches (market requirement) and splits undersize fish back to grow-out.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.1.1: Roller / Bar Grader | Adjustable gap rollers separating fish by girth |
| L4.9.1.2: Chute Assembly | Gravity chutes directing graded fish to bins or transport |
| L4.9.1.3: Counter & Weigher | Optical counter and loadcell providing batch weight and count |

#### L5.9.2: Fish Pump / Transfer

Moves live fish from rearing tanks to grading or slaughter with minimal stress and scale damage.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.2.1: Vacuum / Suction Fish Pump | Gentle-handling vacuum pump; 5–50 t/h transfer capacity |
| L4.9.2.2: Dewatering Screen | Separates transport water from fish at destination |
| L4.9.2.3: Flexible Transfer Hose | Large-bore (100–200 mm) reinforced hose for fish transport |

#### L5.9.3: Stunning & Slaughter

Humane killing per animal welfare standards (electrical, percussive, or CO2 stunning followed by bleeding).

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.3.1: Percussive Stunner | Pneumatic or mechanical blow to head; instant loss of consciousness |
| L4.9.3.2: Electro-Stunner | Electric current applied via water bath or electrodes |
| L4.9.3.3: Bleeding Line & Trough | Gill-cutting station with chilled water trough for bleed-out |

---

sections.title: L6.10 — Water Quality Monitoring & Control (H)
sections.content:
**MARQUARDT PRIMARY POSITION.** Continuous multi-parameter sensing across all rearing and treatment loops. In RAS, flow failure = dissolved oxygen crash = fish mortality within minutes. The flow sensor is the first line of defense for life-safety alarms. Ultrasonic flow sensors have zero moving parts — critical advantage over paddle-wheel sensors that clog with biofilm in recirculating water. [SRC: web_search, 05_market_discovery]

### L5 Systems under L6.10

#### L5.10.1: Flow Measurement

Measures water circulation rate in main loop, individual tank supply, and treatment stages. Flow failure alarm is the most critical safety trigger in RAS.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.10.1.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor | Transit-time flow measurement; no moving parts; no clogging from biofilm. **MARQUARDT PRIMARY POSITION.** DN20/DN25 fits typical RAS piping. |
| L4.10.1.2: Electromagnetic Flow Meter | Mag-meter; higher accuracy but 5–10× cost; used in large commercial RAS |
| L4.10.1.3: Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor | Mechanical incumbent; low cost but clogs with biofilm and particulates in RAS water |

**Alternatives at L4 (Flow Measurement):**

| Alternative | Status | Share Estimate | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paddle-wheel / turbine sensor | Incumbent, declining | 45–55% | MEDIUM |
| No measurement (manual observation) | Legacy, small farms | 15–25% | LOW |
| Electromagnetic flow meter | Established, large farms | 10–20% | MEDIUM |
| Ultrasonic flow sensor | Emerging, growing | 5–10% | LOW |

#### L5.10.2: Dissolved Gas Sensing

Monitors dissolved oxygen (most critical parameter), CO2, and total dissolved gas pressure.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.10.2.1: Dissolved Oxygen Probe | Optical (luminescence) or galvanic DO sensor; continuous inline |
| L4.10.2.2: CO2 Sensor | Nondispersive infrared (NDIR) or pH-based CO2 measurement |
| L4.10.2.3: Total Gas Pressure Sensor | TGP sensor detecting gas supersaturation (causes gas bubble disease) |

#### L5.10.3: Chemical Parameter Sensing

Monitors ammonia, pH, nitrite, and turbidity — the core water chemistry parameters for fish health.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.10.3.1: pH Sensor | Glass or ISFET electrode; inline or submersible |
| L4.10.3.2: Ammonia / TAN Sensor | Ion-selective electrode or colorimetric analyzer for total ammonia nitrogen |
| L4.10.3.3: Turbidity Sensor | Nephelometric turbidity sensor; proxy for suspended solids load |

#### L5.10.4: Central SCADA / Control

Aggregates all sensor data, runs control algorithms, and generates alarms for out-of-range conditions.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.10.4.1: PLC / RTU | Programmable logic controller or remote terminal unit; modbus/BACnet |
| L4.10.4.2: HMI Touch Panel | Local operator interface displaying real-time parameters and trends |
| L4.10.4.3: Alarm Module | SMS/email/siren alarm on flow failure, low DO, high ammonia, or pump failure |

---

sections.title: L6.11 — Biosecurity & Fish Health (H)
sections.content:
Cross-cutting function ensuring disease prevention and containment across all rearing units. A single pathogen introduction can wipe out an entire RAS facility.

### L5 Systems under L6.11

#### L5.11.1: Quarantine System

Isolated water circuit for incoming fish, preventing pathogen introduction to main production.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.11.1.1: Isolation Tank | Separate tank with independent water circuit; no shared equipment |
| L4.11.1.2: Separate Water Circuit | Dedicated filtration, UV, and temperature for quarantine only |
| L4.11.1.3: Barrier Disinfection | Footbaths, hand-wash stations, dedicated nets preventing cross-contamination |

#### L5.11.2: Vaccination & Treatment

Preventive and therapeutic interventions for disease control.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.11.2.1: Immersion Vaccination Bath | Tank for mass vaccination of fingerlings via dip or bath |
| L4.11.2.2: Medicated Feed Dosing | Premix system incorporating medication into feed ration |
| L4.11.2.3: Anesthesia System | MS-222 or clove oil dosing for handling, tagging, or sampling |

#### L5.11.3: Fish Surveillance

Continuous monitoring of fish behavior, mortality, and health indicators for early disease detection.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.11.3.1: Underwater Camera | IP camera monitoring fish behavior, appetite, and physical condition |
| L4.11.3.2: Behavior Analysis Software | AI-driven analysis detecting abnormal swimming patterns or lethargy |
| L4.11.3.3: Mortality Counter | Automated dead-fish detection and counting at tank drain |

---

sections.title: L6.12 — Waste & Effluent Management (H)
sections.content:
RAS produces concentrated waste streams from mechanical filtration backwash, biofilter sludge, and harvest discharge. Regulatory discharge limits (BOD, TSS, TN, TP) require treatment before release.

### L5 Systems under L6.12

#### L5.12.1: Sludge Handling

Collects, concentrates, and removes solid waste from drum filter backwash and settler drains.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.12.1.1: Sludge Collection Tank | Intermediate holding tank for filter backwash and settler discharge |
| L4.12.1.2: Dewatering Press / Belt Filter | Screw press or belt filter concentrating sludge to 15–25% dry matter |
| L4.12.1.3: Sludge Pump | Progressive cavity pump transferring sludge to dewatering |

#### L5.12.2: Effluent Treatment

Treats liquid discharge to meet environmental permit requirements before release.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.12.2.1: Settling Pond | Gravity settling of remaining suspended solids |
| L4.12.2.2: Constructed Wetland | Planted wetland for nutrient polishing (nitrogen, phosphorus removal) |
| L4.12.2.3: pH Neutralization Unit | Chemical dosing to adjust effluent pH to permitted range |

#### L5.12.3: Nutrient Recovery

Converts waste nutrients into value-added by-products (fertilizer, compost, aquaponics crops).

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.12.3.1: Solids Separator | Vibrating screen or hydrocyclone separating coarse and fine solids |
| L4.12.3.2: Compost System | Aerobic composting of dewatered fish sludge into fertilizer |
| L4.12.3.3: Aquaponics Interface | Water/nutrient delivery to hydroponic plant beds using RAS effluent |

---

sections.title: L6.13 — Facility Utilities & Power (H)
sections.content:
RAS facilities require uninterrupted power — a 15-minute power outage without backup can cause total stock loss. Emergency generators and UPS for control systems are mandatory.

### L5 Systems under L6.13

#### L5.13.1: Emergency Power

Backup electrical supply ensuring water circulation and oxygenation continue during grid failure.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.13.1.1: Diesel Generator | Automatic-start generator sized for full-facility critical loads |
| L4.13.1.2: Automatic Transfer Switch | ATS switching to generator within 10–30 seconds of grid loss |
| L4.13.1.3: UPS for Controls | Battery UPS maintaining PLC/SCADA and alarm systems during transfer |

#### L5.13.2: Compressed Air / Blower System

Provides air for aeration, MBBR agitation, pneumatic valve actuation, and ozone generation.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.13.2.1: Regenerative Blower | High-volume low-pressure blower for aeration and MBBR |
| L4.13.2.2: Air Distribution Manifold | Header with individual zone valves and flow regulators |
| L4.13.2.3: Pressure Regulator | Maintains consistent air pressure at point of use |

#### L5.13.3: Building Services

Facility-level infrastructure supporting the production environment.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.13.3.1: Ventilation System | Exhaust fans and fresh air intake preventing humidity buildup and CO2 accumulation |

- Flow-through/raceway skips mechanical and biological filtration (continuous water exchange replaces recirculation treatment)
- Pond systems have minimal equipment beyond rearing, feed, and harvest
- **Marquardt sensor relevance is strongest in RAS and Hatchery** where closed-loop flow monitoring is life-critical

sections.title: Marquardt Sensor Position Analysis
sections.content:
### Primary L-Path

**L7** Finfish Aquaculture Ecosystem → **L6.10** Water Quality Monitoring & Control (H) → **L5.10.1** Flow Measurement → **L4.10.1.1** Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

### Why This Position

| Factor | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Life-safety criticality** | Flow failure in RAS = DO crash = fish die within minutes. Flow sensor is the first alarm trigger. |
| **Biofilm resistance** | No moving parts — critical advantage. Paddle-wheel sensors clog within weeks in RAS water with biofilm, particulates, and organic matter. |
| **Pipe diameter fit** | RAS main loops typically DN20–DN50; individual tank feeds DN15–DN25. Marquardt DN20/DN25 covers tank-level and small-loop applications directly. |
| **Price advantage** | 5–10× cheaper than electromagnetic flowmeters. Price-sensitive aquaculture operators currently choose paddle-wheel or no measurement — ultrasonic fills the gap. |
| **Anomaly detection value** | Empty-pipe and air-bubble detection provides additional safety layer — detects pump failure or air lock before DO drops. |
| **Temperature co-measurement** | Integrated NTC provides rearing water temperature alongside flow — eliminates separate temperature probe at this measurement point. |

### Secondary Positions

| L6 | L5 | L4 | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| L6.6 Temperature Control | L5.6.1 Heat Exchanger (monitoring loop) | Flow Sensor on heating/cooling water circuit | Verify heat exchanger flow rate; detect fouling via flow degradation |
| L6.1 Water Intake & Conditioning | L5.1.1 Source Water Pump Station | Intake flow metering | Makeup water volume measurement for water budget tracking |

### Competitive Landscape at L4 (Flow Measurement in Aquaculture)

| Alternative | Status | Share | Confidence | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paddle-wheel sensor | Incumbent | 45–55% | MEDIUM | Very low cost ($20–80); simple | Clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks; moving parts wear; no anomaly detection |
| No measurement | Legacy | 15–25% | LOW | Zero cost | Blind to flow failure; relies on downstream DO alarm or fish mortality |
| Electromagnetic flowmeter | Established | 10–20% | MEDIUM | High accuracy (±0.5%); no clogging | 5–10× cost ($500–2,000+); overkill for most farm-scale applications |
| Ultrasonic transit-time | Emerging | 5–10% | LOW | No moving parts; low maintenance; compact; anomaly detection | Emerging awareness in aquaculture; requires acoustic coupling with water (met by default in RAS) |

[SRC: web_search, EST based on aquaculture equipment distributor catalogs]

---

*End of VN — feeds JTBD Analysis, Competitive Landscape, and Fit Assessment for the Finfish Farming market. The Marquardt primary L-path (L7 > L6.10 > L5.10.1 > L4.10.1.1) is the anchor for all downstream coverage and ODI scoring. Secondary positions (temperature control loop, water intake metering) provide additional volume per RAS installation. Whitespace opportunity: ultrasonic is virtually unknown in aquaculture today — the sensor's biofilm-proof, no-moving-parts design addresses the industry's #1 flow measurement pain point.*

rows.Assessment: Flow failure in RAS = DO crash = fish die within minutes. Flow sensor is the first alarm trigger.
No moving parts — critical advantage. Paddle-wheel sensors clog within weeks in RAS water with biofilm, particulates, and organic matter.
RAS main loops typically DN20–DN50; individual tank feeds DN15–DN25. Marquardt DN20/DN25 covers tank-level and small-loop applications directly.
5–10× cheaper than electromagnetic flowmeters. Price-sensitive aquaculture operators currently choose paddle-wheel or no measurement — ultrasonic fills the gap.
Empty-pipe and air-bubble detection provides additional safety layer — detects pump failure or air lock before DO drops.
Integrated NTC provides rearing water temperature alongside flow — eliminates separate temperature probe at this measurement point.

rows.L4: Flow Sensor on heating/cooling water circuit
rows.Rationale: Verify heat exchanger flow rate; detect fouling via flow degradation
Makeup water volume measurement for water budget tracking

rows.Cons: Clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks; moving parts wear; no anomaly detection
Blind to flow failure; relies on downstream DO alarm or fish mortality
5–10× cost ($500–2,000+); overkill for most farm-scale applications

rows.Pros: No moving parts; low maintenance; compact; anomaly detection
rows.Cons: Emerging awareness in aquaculture; requires acoustic coupling with water (met by default in RAS)

secondary_positions.l4: Flow Sensor on heating/cooling water circuit

marquardt_position.whitespace_assessment: Ultrasonic flow sensors are a WHITESPACE opportunity in aquaculture. Most farms use paddle-wheel (clogs with biofilm) or no measurement at all. The Marquardt sensor's zero-clog, no-moving-parts design addresses the #1 pain point. Price point ($50-150 OEM) is 5-10x below electromagnetic but provides superior reliability vs paddle-wheel in RAS water with biofilm and particulates.
marquardt_position.cost_of_failure: Flow failure in RAS = dissolved oxygen crash = mass fish mortality within minutes. A single flow sensor failure at a 500-t/year salmon RAS can cause $2-5M in stock loss. The flow sensor is the cheapest life-safety device in the system.
